one_line_takeaway: MalTool shows that an attacker can use an iterative generate-and-verify loop around any coding LLM (safety-aligned or not) to automatically synthesize malicious MCP tools with a 100% verified-success rate at sub-$0.05 cost per tool, and that current malware/code scanners (VirusTotal, Bandit, Semgrep, and three MCP-specific scanners) fail to reliably catch these tools without also swamping benign tools in false positives.
key_finding: MalTool with the verifier achieves a Generation Success Rate (GSR) of 1.000 across all 13 behaviors and all three open-weight coding LLMs (GPT-OSS-20B, Phi-4, Qwen3-Coder-30B), versus highly variable GSR without the verifier (e.g., as low as 0.020 for Phi-4 on CPU Compute Hijacking, 0.120 for Phi-4 on File Encryption). It also reduces structural similarity (SIM) relative to the no-verifier baseline in every cell of Table 2. Verifier feedback substantially cuts generation-verification iterations needed to reach an accepted tool, e.g. on Phi-4/CPU Compute Hijacking from 77.289 iterations without feedback to 6.500 with feedback. Safety alignment does not block the attack: safety-aligned versions of the open-weight models and all four closed-source models also reach GSR=1.000 on every behavior, at a low per-successful-tool cost of about $0.013 (GPT-4o), $0.017 (GPT-5.2), $0.033 (Claude-Opus-4.6), $0.016 (Gemini-3-Pro). On detection, existing detectors fail broadly: VirusTotal has FNR=1.000 (or near it) on essentially every behavior in both Dataset I and Dataset II; the best-performing single detector varies by behavior, but Resource Hijacking and Denial-of-Service behaviors are 'near-universally' evaded (FNR near 1.0 across most detectors); even the Combined Scanner leaves substantial FNR on Data Poisoning, Resource Hijacking, and Denial of Service (e.g., FNR 0.850 for GPU Compute Hijacking, 0.760 for File Encryption on Dataset I). On benign Dataset III (4,846 tools, 1,147 repos), VirusTotal has FPR=0 but Tencent A.I.G and AntGroup MCPScan frequently flag benign tools (FPR often 30-50%+), and the Combined Scanner has the highest FPR, often exceeding 50% and over 70% in some categories -- i.e., high-recall aggregation trades away precision.
core_contribution: The paper (Hu, Jia, Li, Song, Gong; Duke University and UC Berkeley) presents the first systematic study of the code-level implementation of malicious tool attacks against LLM agents, arguing that prior work only addressed how attackers get a malicious tool installed and selected (via crafted names/descriptions and prompt injection), while the third necessary condition -- actually embedding malicious behavior in the tool's code -- had not been studied. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a taxonomy of 13 malicious tool behaviors organized under the confidentiality-integrity-availability (CIA) triad, tailored to LLM-agent settings (e.g., exfiltrating agent memory/knowledge base, credential harvesting/abuse, GPU compute hijacking); (2) MalTool, a coding-LLM-based framework that automatically synthesizes 'standalone' malicious tools (a single malicious function) and 'Trojan' malicious tools (malicious logic embedded into an otherwise-benign real-world tool) via an iterative generate-and-verify loop with an execution-based correctness verifier and an AST-subtree Jaccard-similarity diversity verifier; (3) two constructed datasets -- Dataset I (1,300 standalone malicious tools, 100 per behavior across 13 behaviors) and Dataset II (5,727 Trojan malicious tools embedded into real-world MCP tools, ~440-441 per behavior) -- plus Dataset III (4,846 benign real-world tools reserved for false-positive evaluation); and (4) an empirical evaluation of six existing malicious-tool/malware detectors (VirusTotal, Bandit, Semgrep, Tencent A.I.G, Cisco MCP Scanner, AntGroup MCPScan, plus a 'Combined Scanner') on both datasets, showing they suffer simultaneously high false-negative and false-positive rates.
threat_model: Attacker is a malicious tool developer who uploads a tool to a public distribution platform (mcp.so, mcpservers, skillsmp). The attacker's goal is realized only if: (1) a user installs the tool (often via a deceptive name/description), (2) an LLM agent selects it during task execution, and (3) the tool's code implementation carries out harmful behavior once invoked. This paper explicitly narrows its scope to condition (3), citing conditions (1)-(2) as covered by prior work (Shi et al. 2024/2026, Fu et al. 2024) on tool-name/description manipulation and prompt injection. Attacker background knowledge: familiarity with tool protocols (MCP, Skills) and access to a coding LLM (open-weight, safety-ablated or safety-aligned, or closed-source e.g. GPT-4o/GPT-5.2/Claude-Opus-4.6/Gemini-3-Pro); the attacker does NOT have access to the victim's specific LLM agent or the user's tasks. Attacker capability: can craft tool metadata to look benign, embed prompt injections in descriptions, and embed malicious logic either as a standalone malicious tool or trojanned into an otherwise-functional benign tool; once invoked, the tool inherits the agent's file-system privileges, including access to agent memory, knowledge base, and user credentials (e.g., stored in a .env file). The paper explicitly contrasts this with traditional malware: malicious tools are invoked through the agent's normal tool-selection process rather than through exploiting a system vulnerability.
limitations: The authors state these themselves: (1) Dataset III (benign tools) may contain a small subset of actually-malicious tools missed during collection, though they argue systemic misclassification (not undiscovered malware) explains the high FPRs, and cross-check this against a concurrent measurement study (Liu et al. 2026) reporting low prevalence of malicious tools in the wild; (2) the 13 malicious behaviors and their verifier instantiations use synthetic data and controlled/local infrastructure and do not cover the full spectrum of possible attack instantiations in real LLM-agent deployments; (3) detection methods evaluated are limited to code/metadata-analysis-based tools -- the paper does not evaluate defenses based on joint text-code semantic-consistency analysis or runtime guardrails, which it flags as future work; (4) Dataset II's Trojan tools were built only from standalone malicious tools generated by GPT-OSS-20B (not the full model set) embedded into real-world tools scraped from three MCP platforms with Python/fastmcp entry points, so generality to other languages/protocols is untested.

datasets_used: Dataset I: 1,300 standalone malicious tools (100 per behavior x 13 behaviors), synthesized by MalTool
Dataset II: 5,727 Trojan malicious tools (~440-441 per behavior x 13 behaviors), built by embedding Dataset I malicious functions (from GPT-OSS-20B) into real-world tools
Dataset III: 4,846 benign real-world tools across 1,147 repositories, reserved from the same 10,573-tool real-world collection, used to measure detector false-positive rate

10,573 real-world tools collected from mcp.so, mcpservers, and mcpmarket (source: public GitHub repos, Python/fastmcp entry points), split into the 5,727 used for Dataset II and 4,846 reserved as Dataset III
quantitative_results: GSR (Generation Success Rate) with verifier = 1.000 across all 13 behaviors x {GPT-OSS-20B, Phi-4, Qwen3-Coder-30B}; without verifier ranges as low as 0.020 (Phi-4, CPU Compute Hijacking) up to 1.000 (e.g. GPT-OSS-20B, Database Record Deletion)
Average iterations to acceptance drop with verifier feedback, e.g. Phi-4/CPU Compute Hijacking: 77.289 (w/o feedback) -> 6.500 (w/ feedback); Qwen3-Coder-30B/File Encryption: 83.333 -> 6.594

Cost per successful malicious tool generation: $0.013 GPT-4o, $0.017 GPT-5.2, $0.033 Claude-Opus-4.6, $0.016 Gemini-3-Pro; safety-aligned models also reach GSR=1.000 on all behaviors, with average iterations rising slightly from 2.508 to 2.944 and SIM roughly stable (0.166 vs 0.154)
VirusTotal FNR on Dataset I: 1.000 on 12 of 13 behaviors, 0.980 on CPU Compute Hijacking
Combined Scanner FNR on Dataset I ranges from 0.000 (Credential Abuse) to 0.850 (GPU Compute Hijacking) and 0.760 (File Encryption)
On Dataset II (Trojan tools, n=440-441 per behavior), Combined Scanner FNR ranges from 0.005 (File Encryption) to 0.215 (GPU Compute Hijacking)

FPR on Dataset III (benign, repository-level, n=1,147 repos): VirusTotal = 0.000 across all categories; Combined Scanner FPR ranges roughly 0.586-0.762 across categories (e.g. 0.612 Productivity, 0.762 Development, 0.733 File System)
88% of collected real-world tools have fewer than 100 lines of code (LOC)
Manual validation: 130 sampled Trojan tools manually executed, 100% preserved advertised benign functionality while triggering the embedded malicious behavior
baselines_compared: w/o verifier: direct prompting of the coding LLM with the system prompt but no correctness/diversity verification or feedback loop
w/o feedback: uses the verifier but the system prompt does not incorporate verifier feedback into later generations

Detection baselines: VirusTotal (commercial malware scanner), Bandit and Semgrep (static program-analysis/SAST tools), Tencent A.I.G, Cisco MCP Scanner, and AntGroup MCPScan (agent/MCP-tailored code scanners), and a 'Combined Scanner' aggregating all of the above
